tasks.title: Warm-up: Create a Simple Job and Observe the Completion Flow
tasks.goal: Write the simplest possible Job manifest, apply it, and observe the Pod transitioning from Running to Completed. Learn the restartPolicy constraint and how to inspect Job and Pod logs.
tasks.prompt:
Write a Job manifest (hello-job.yaml) that satisfies the following requirements and apply it.

Requirements:
- Job name: hello-job
- Image: busybox:1.36
- Command: sh -c 'echo "Job started: $(date)"; sleep 5; echo "Job done"'
- restartPolicy: Never

After applying, verify the state in the following order:
1. Run kubectl get jobs repeatedly and confirm COMPLETIONS changes from 0/1 to 1/1.
2. Run kubectl get pods and confirm the Pod is in Completed status.
3. Run kubectl logs job/hello-job and confirm the container output.
tasks.starter:
# hello-job.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: ____
metadata:
  name: hello-job
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: hello
          image: busybox:1.36
          command: ["sh", "-c", "echo 'Job started: $(date)'; sleep 5; echo 'Job done'"]
      restartPolicy: ____  # choose Never or OnFailure
tasks.solution:
# hello-job.yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: hello-job
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: hello
          image: busybox:1.36
          command: ["sh", "-c", "echo 'Job started: $(date)'; sleep 5; echo 'Job done'"]
      restartPolicy: Never

# Apply
kubectl apply -f hello-job.yaml

# Check status (completion takes ~5-10 seconds)
kubectl get jobs
# NAME        COMPLETIONS   DURATION   AGE
# hello-job   1/1           8s         12s

kubectl get pods
# NAME              READY   STATUS      RESTARTS   AGE
# hello-job-xxxxx   0/1     Completed   0          15s

# Inspect logs
kubectl logs job/hello-job
# Expected output:
# Job started: Mon May 25 ...
# Job done
tasks.explanation: Jobs belong to the batch/v1 API group, so the manifest must specify apiVersion: batch/v1. Setting restartPolicy to Always is rejected immediately by the API server (it is only valid for Deployments). With Never, the controller creates a new Pod to retry when the container fails; with OnFailure, only the container is restarted within the same Pod. Completed Pods are not deleted automatically — they remain in Completed state so their output can be retrieved with kubectl logs. This is fundamentally different from a Deployment Pod, which would be restarted.
hints: The apiVersion is batch/v1, not v1. If kubectl apply returns 'no matches for kind Job in version v1', check apiVersion.
Using restartPolicy: Always triggers an 'Unsupported value: "Always"' validation error. Only Never or OnFailure are accepted for Jobs.
kubectl logs job/hello-job automatically locates the Pod created by the Job — you do not need to know the Pod name.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 1: Build a Job with completions, parallelism, and backoffLimit
tasks.goal: Write a Job with completions: 4 and parallelism: 2, observe that Pods execute in batches of two, then write a second Job with a low backoffLimit and an always-failing command to see how the retry limit behaves.
tasks.prompt:
Part A — Parallel Job observation:
Write a Job manifest (parallel-job.yaml) with the following requirements:
- Job name: parallel-job
- completions: 4  (4 successful completions required)
- parallelism: 2  (run 2 Pods at a time)
- Image: busybox:1.36
- Command: sh -c 'echo "Pod $(hostname) processing"; sleep 8'
- restartPolicy: Never

After applying, use kubectl get pods -w to watch Pods execute in batches of two in real time.
After completion, confirm COMPLETIONS shows 4/4 in kubectl get jobs.

Part B — backoffLimit behavior:
Write a Job (fail-job.yaml) with the following requirements:
- Job name: fail-job
- backoffLimit: 2  (Job is marked Failed after 2 retries)
- Image: busybox:1.36
- Command: sh -c 'echo "simulating failure"; exit 1'  (always fails)
- restartPolicy: Never

After applying, use kubectl get jobs -w to observe the state, and confirm the Job ends as Failed with COMPLETIONS still at 0/1.
tasks.starter:
# parallel-job.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: parallel-job
spec:
  completions: ____
  parallelism: ____
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: worker
          image: busybox:1.36
          command: ["sh", "-c", "echo 'Pod $(hostname) processing'; sleep 8"]
      restartPolicy: Never

---
# fail-job.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: fail-job
spec:
  backoffLimit: ____
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: worker
          image: busybox:1.36
          command: ["sh", "-c", "echo 'simulating failure'; exit 1"]
      restartPolicy: Never
tasks.solution:
# parallel-job.yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: parallel-job
spec:
  completions: 4
  parallelism: 2
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: worker
          image: busybox:1.36
          command: ["sh", "-c", "echo 'Pod $(hostname) processing'; sleep 8"]
      restartPolicy: Never

# Apply and watch in real time
kubectl apply -f parallel-job.yaml
kubectl get pods -w
# First 2 Pods (parallel-job-xxxxx, parallel-job-yyyyy) start simultaneously → Completed
# Next 2 Pods start → Completed
# kubectl get jobs shows COMPLETIONS 4/4

kubectl get jobs
# NAME           COMPLETIONS   DURATION   AGE
# parallel-job   4/4           20s        35s

---
# fail-job.yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: fail-job
spec:
  backoffLimit: 2
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: worker
          image: busybox:1.36
          command: ["sh", "-c", "echo 'simulating failure'; exit 1"]
      restartPolicy: Never

# Apply and observe
kubectl apply -f fail-job.yaml
kubectl get jobs -w
# NAME       COMPLETIONS   DURATION   AGE
# fail-job   0/1           5s         5s
# fail-job   0/1           ...        ...
# (after 3 attempts → Failed)

# Confirm final state
kubectl describe job fail-job
# Conditions: Type=Failed, Reason=BackoffLimitExceeded

# List the failed Pods
kubectl get pods -l job-name=fail-job
# 3 Pods all in Error state (backoffLimit 2 → 3 total attempts: 1 initial + 2 retries)
tasks.explanation:
Understanding the relationship between completions and parallelism is the heart of this exercise. completions is 'how many successes are needed in total'; parallelism is 'how many Pods run at the same time'. With completions: 4 and parallelism: 2, execution proceeds as (2 concurrent Pods complete) × 2 batches. The kubectl get pods -w output lets you see both Pods reach Completed state before the next pair starts.

For backoffLimit: the Job controller creates a new Pod for each failure and retries until the attempt count reaches backoffLimit, at which point it marks the Job as Failed and stops creating new Pods. With backoffLimit: 2, the total number of Pods created is 3 (1 initial attempt + 2 retries). The BackoffLimitExceeded reason is visible in the Conditions field of kubectl describe job fail-job.
hints: Run kubectl get pods -w in one terminal and kubectl apply in another to watch Pod creation in real time. The -w (watch) flag streams updates as they happen.
With backoffLimit: 2, three Pods will accumulate in Error state (1 initial + 2 retries). Confirm with kubectl get pods -l job-name=fail-job.
While parallel-job is running, notice that each Pod has a unique hash suffix in its name. This is because the Job creates a brand-new Pod for every completion unit.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 2: Create a CronJob and Test concurrencyPolicy, suspend, and Manual Triggering
tasks.goal: Create a CronJob and observe the delegation structure in which Jobs are created automatically. Experience all three operational features: concurrencyPolicy Forbid, suspend, and manual triggering with kubectl create job --from=cronjob.
tasks.prompt:
Write a CronJob manifest (heartbeat-cj.yaml) that meets the following requirements and apply it.

Requirements:
- CronJob name: heartbeat-cj
- schedule: '*/1 * * * *'  (every 1 minute)
- concurrencyPolicy: Forbid  (skip new Job if previous one is still running)
- successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
- failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1
- Image: busybox:1.36
- Command: sh -c 'echo "heartbeat $(date)"'
- restartPolicy: OnFailure

After applying, perform the following steps in order:

1. Wait 1-2 minutes, then use kubectl get cronjob and kubectl get jobs to confirm Jobs are being created automatically.
2. Run kubectl create job --from=cronjob/heartbeat-cj manual-run-1 to trigger an immediate one-time run without waiting for the schedule.
3. Run kubectl logs job/manual-run-1 to confirm the output.
4. Run kubectl patch cronjob heartbeat-cj -p '{"spec":{"suspend":true}}' to pause the CronJob.
5. Confirm the SUSPEND column shows True in kubectl get cronjob heartbeat-cj.
6. Run kubectl patch cronjob heartbeat-cj -p '{"spec":{"suspend":false}}' to resume.
tasks.starter:
# heartbeat-cj.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: heartbeat-cj
spec:
  schedule: "____"          # cron expression for every 1 minute
  concurrencyPolicy: ____   # Forbid: wait for previous Job to finish before creating a new one
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: ____
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: ____
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: heartbeat
              image: busybox:1.36
              command: ["sh", "-c", "echo 'heartbeat $(date)'"]
          restartPolicy: ____
tasks.solution:
# heartbeat-cj.yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: heartbeat-cj
spec:
  schedule: "*/1 * * * *"
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: heartbeat
              image: busybox:1.36
              command: ["sh", "-c", "echo 'heartbeat $(date)'"]
          restartPolicy: OnFailure

# Apply
kubectl apply -f heartbeat-cj.yaml

# 1. Confirm automatic execution (wait 1-2 minutes)
kubectl get cronjob heartbeat-cj
# NAME           SCHEDULE      SUSPEND   ACTIVE   LAST SCHEDULE   AGE
# heartbeat-cj   */1 * * * *   False     0        32s             2m

kubectl get jobs
# NAME                        COMPLETIONS   DURATION   AGE
# heartbeat-cj-28xxxxxx       1/1           5s         90s

# 2. Manual immediate trigger
kubectl create job --from=cronjob/heartbeat-cj manual-run-1

# 3. Inspect manual run logs
kubectl logs job/manual-run-1
# Expected output: heartbeat Mon May 25 ...

# 4. Pause
kubectl patch cronjob heartbeat-cj -p '{"spec":{"suspend":true}}'

# 5. Confirm SUSPEND column
kubectl get cronjob heartbeat-cj
# NAME           SCHEDULE      SUSPEND   ACTIVE   LAST SCHEDULE   AGE
# heartbeat-cj   */1 * * * *   True      0        1m              5m

# 6. Resume
kubectl patch cronjob heartbeat-cj -p '{"spec":{"suspend":false}}'
kubectl get cronjob heartbeat-cj
# Confirm SUSPEND column has returned to False
tasks.explanation:
A CronJob is a wrapper that contains a complete Job spec under jobTemplate. The CronJob itself does nothing — its sole role is to create a new Job at each scheduled time. In kubectl get jobs output, you can see Jobs named in the pattern 'heartbeat-cj-28xxxxxx', showing that the CronJob automatically assigned names and created them.

concurrencyPolicy: Forbid skips creating a new Job when the previous one is still running at the next scheduled time. This must be set for workloads like database backups where two concurrent processes touching the same resource would cause corruption.

kubectl create job --from=cronjob is the essential operational command for triggering an immediate test run without waiting for the next scheduled time. The suspend true/false patch pauses and resumes Job creation without deleting the CronJob — useful for temporarily halting a CronJob during emergency maintenance.
hints: The schedule expression '*/1 * * * *' means 'every multiple of 1 minute', which is every minute. Writing a plain '*' is equivalent. Paste it into crontab.guru and it reads 'Every minute'.
If no automatically created Jobs appear in kubectl get jobs, not enough time has passed yet. If LAST SCHEDULE shows <none> in kubectl get cronjob heartbeat-cj, wait a little longer.

In the kubectl patch command, the JSON string's quotes may be interpreted by the shell. On Windows PowerShell, you may need to escape the inner double quotes instead of using single quotes: kubectl patch cronjob heartbeat-cj -p '{"spec":{"suspend":true}}'

tasks.title: Advanced: Deploy a DaemonSet and Verify Per-Node Placement and Toleration Behavior
tasks.goal: Write a DaemonSet manifest from scratch and confirm that one Pod is deployed on every node. Add a toleration to also schedule Pods on the control-plane node, and understand the auto-scaling characteristic of DaemonSet.
tasks.prompt:
Part A — Basic DaemonSet deployment:
Write a DaemonSet manifest (node-checker.yaml) with the following requirements and apply it.
- DaemonSet name: node-checker
- selector.matchLabels: app: node-checker
- Image: busybox:1.36
- Command: sh -c 'while true; do echo "$(hostname -f) running $(date)"; sleep 30; done'
- hostPath volume: mount the node's /etc/os-release to /host/os-release in the container as readOnly

After applying:
1. Run kubectl get daemonset and check the DESIRED/CURRENT/READY values.
2. Run kubectl get pods -o wide and confirm each Pod is on a different node (NODE column).
3. Run kubectl exec <pod-name> -- cat /host/os-release to verify the node OS information is accessible.

Part B — Add toleration:
Add the following toleration to node-checker.yaml and re-apply with kubectl apply:
- key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
- operator: Exists
- effect: NoSchedule

After re-applying, confirm that the DESIRED count in kubectl get daemonset has increased (now includes the control-plane node).
tasks.starter:
# node-checker.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: node-checker
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ____
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ____
    spec:
      # location for Part B
      # tolerations:
      #   - key: ____
      #     operator: ____
      #     effect: ____
      containers:
        - name: checker
          image: busybox:1.36
          command: ["sh", "-c", "while true; do echo '$(hostname -f) running $(date)'; sleep 30; done"]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: os-info
              mountPath: ____
              readOnly: true
      volumes:
        - name: os-info
          hostPath:
            path: ____
tasks.solution:
# node-checker.yaml (Part A)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: node-checker
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: node-checker
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: node-checker
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: checker
          image: busybox:1.36
          command: ["sh", "-c", "while true; do echo '$(hostname -f) running $(date)'; sleep 30; done"]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: os-info
              mountPath: /host/os-release
              readOnly: true
      volumes:
        - name: os-info
          hostPath:
            path: /etc/os-release

# Apply
kubectl apply -f node-checker.yaml

# 1. DaemonSet status
kubectl get daemonset
# NAME           DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   NODE SELECTOR   AGE
# node-checker   2         2         2       2            2           <none>          30s
# (example with 2 worker nodes)

# 2. Per-node placement
kubectl get pods -o wide
# NAME                 READY   STATUS    ... NODE
# node-checker-abc12   1/1     Running   ... node-1
# node-checker-def34   1/1     Running   ... node-2

# 3. Verify hostPath mount
kubectl exec node-checker-abc12 -- cat /host/os-release
# NAME="Ubuntu"
# VERSION="22.04.3 LTS (Jammy Jellyfish)"
# ...

---
# node-checker.yaml (Part B: add tolerations)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: node-checker
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: node-checker
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: node-checker
    spec:
      tolerations:
        - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
          operator: Exists
          effect: NoSchedule
      containers:
        - name: checker
          image: busybox:1.36
          command: ["sh", "-c", "while true; do echo '$(hostname -f) running $(date)'; sleep 30; done"]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: os-info
              mountPath: /host/os-release
              readOnly: true
      volumes:
        - name: os-info
          hostPath:
            path: /etc/os-release

# Re-apply and verify
kubectl apply -f node-checker.yaml
kubectl get daemonset
# DESIRED increases by 1 to include the control-plane node

kubectl get pods -o wide
# NODE column now includes the control-plane node name
tasks.explanation:
DaemonSet has no replica field. Instead, the number of nodes in the cluster determines the desired Pod count (DESIRED). When kubectl get daemonset shows DESIRED equal to the number of nodes, the DaemonSet is healthy. Adding a node automatically increments DESIRED; removing a node removes the Pod on it automatically.

The hostPath volume mounts the node's actual filesystem path directly into the container. Log agents and metrics collectors use this pattern to access node-level files such as /var/log or /proc.

Control-plane nodes carry a NoSchedule taint by default, which prevents ordinary Pods from being scheduled there. Adding a toleration declares that the DaemonSet is willing to 'tolerate' that taint, allowing it to cover every node without exception. After applying Part B, if DESIRED increases by 1, the Pod has been scheduled on the control-plane node. On a single-node minikube cluster (where the node acts as both control-plane and worker), DESIRED is always 1 regardless of the toleration.
hints: If kubectl get daemonset shows DESIRED as 0, either no nodes are ready or all nodes have taints that are not tolerated. Run kubectl get nodes first to check node status.

In a hostPath mount, specifying a file path (like /host/os-release) as mountPath mounts a file; specifying a directory path mounts a directory. Since path: /etc/os-release is a file, mountPath must also be a file path for kubectl exec -- cat to work correctly.

If DESIRED does not increase after applying Part B, check the actual taint key on your control-plane node with kubectl describe node <control-plane-node> | grep Taint. If the key is different (e.g., node-role.kubernetes.io/master), update the toleration key accordingly.
